message:
fix(hook,update): replace three permissive substring matchers with anchored / basename-based equality

Three separate matcher-correctness bugs, all of the same shape: a lax
substring / prefix / regex match let unrelated strings silently satisfy
a check that was meant to identify a single specific artifact. Fixing
them together because they hit the same audit thread.

1. rch/src/agent/hook.rs — `cmd.contains("rch")` -> `is_rch_hook_command`

   `check_*_hook` / `install_*_hook` / `uninstall_*_hook` identified which
   hook entries belong to rch by scanning the Claude Code / Gemini CLI /
   Continue.dev settings JSON for entries whose `command` string
   *contained* the literal "rch". That matches legitimate unrelated
   hooks whose command basename is anything like `search`, `archive`,
   `rearchive`, `arch`, `rch-wkr`, `rchd`, `myrchwrapper`, `rchextra`,
   etc. — and in `uninstall_*_hook` that meant `rch hook uninstall`
   could *silently delete* those unrelated hooks from the user's
   settings file.

   Fix: new `is_rch_hook_command(cmd)` helper that takes the first
   whitespace-separated token, lifts its basename via
   `Path::file_name()`, and compares for exact equality with `"rch"`.
   Handles bare name, absolute path, and trailing args. Called at all
   seven match sites (check, install, uninstall across Claude Code,
   Gemini CLI, Continue.dev, and two test assertions).

   New regression tests: `is_rch_hook_command_matches_bare_name`,
   `matches_absolute_path`, `matches_with_args`,
   `rejects_substring_false_positives` (pins all the substrings the
   old contains() would have mis-matched on), `rejects_empty`.

2. rch/src/update/download.rs — `ends_with("/filename")` -> basename compare

   SHA256SUMS-style checksum files list "HASH  path" lines. The matcher
   accepted a line when the path column equaled the target filename OR
   ended with `/filename` OR `\filename`. The ends_with form is
   prefix-injectable: a crafted line like
     `EVIL_HASH  ../evil/rch-v1.0.0-linux.tar.gz`
   would satisfy `ends_with("/rch-v1.0.0-linux.tar.gz")` and let the
   caller accept `EVIL_HASH` for the release asset. Worse, a line
   `ATTACKER_HASH  pfxrch-v0.1.0-linux.tar.gz` would have been rejected
   by the `/` guard but the more dangerous form above slipped past.

   Fix: extract the basename via `Path::file_name()` and also strip any
   Windows-style `\`-separated trailing component, compare both against
   the target filename for exact equality. No more partial-path acceptance.

   New regression tests: `test_extract_checksum_rejects_nondelimited_suffix`
   (pins the attacker-hash concatenation case) and
   `test_extract_checksum_windows_backslash_basename`.

3. rch/src/update/verify.rs — anchor + escape cosign identity regex

   `RCH_RELEASE_IDENTITY_PATTERN` was passed to cosign's
   `--certificate-identity-regexp`, which under the hood calls Go's
   `regexp.MatchString` — which is SUBSTRING-matching, not full-match.
   The pattern was unanchored AND used bare `.` to separate
   `github.com`, `.github`, and `.yml`. A malicious certificate whose
   SAN merely contained a URL that textually includes our workflow path
   as a substring (e.g. nested inside a longer identity URL) would
   satisfy the check, defeating the release-binding guarantee.

   Fix: raw-string, anchor `^...$`, escape every literal `.` in
   `github.com`, `.github`, `release.yml`. Now only a certificate whose
   SAN exactly matches the workflow path can satisfy the check. Kept
   the `@refs/.*` suffix permissive — any ref the release workflow
   signs under is still accepted, which is the intended flexibility.

All three fixes are of the "was too lenient, now exactly as strict as
the docstring claims" flavor. No call-sites changed behavior for the
legitimate inputs; only previously-accepted attacker-shaped inputs are
now rejected.
